name: International Conference on Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS)
description: The oldest dedicated ITS/LE conference, founded 1988 in Montreal. Biennial; proceedings published by Springer LNCS. Premier venue for cognitive models, student modeling, adaptive instruction, and feedback algorithm research. ITS and AIED now alternate years, making one or the other the annual flagship AI-in-education conference.

name: International Conference on Learning Analytics and Knowledge (LAK)
description: Practice-oriented learning analytics, ethical deployment, dashboard design, predictive modeling, algorithmic fairness. Community-building for the SoLAR network. Emphasizes socio-technical context of analytics; complements EDM's algorithmic focus.

name: International Conference on Educational Data Mining (EDM)
description: Algorithmic and computational approaches to educational data: knowledge tracing, early warning systems, automated feature engineering, student behavior modeling. One of three major computational LE conference series (with LAK and AIED).

name: International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED)
description: AI-driven educational systems: generative AI tutors, NLP for learning, deep knowledge tracing, conversational agents, human-AI interaction in education. Largest first-authorship from China — highlights globally distributed LE research ecosystem.

name: International Society of the Learning Sciences Annual Meeting (ISLS/ICLS)
description: Design-based research, socio-cultural learning theory, collaborative learning, epistemology of learning environments. Balances computational rigor with qualitative and humanistic perspectives. Provides the socio-cultural counterbalance to EDM/LAK's algorithmic orientation.

name: Learning @ Scale (L@S)
description: Technical and pedagogical research on large-scale digital learning: MOOCs, online education platforms, at-scale experimentation, A/B testing in learning contexts. ACM-sponsored; focuses on the intersection of scale and learning quality. Home of 'doer effect' and A/B experimentation papers.

name: IEEE ICICLE Annual Meeting
description: Annual community gathering for the IEEE learning engineering community. Standards, BoK, and credentialing discussions. Field-defining community event for LE professionals.

name: ACM CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
description: Premier venue for human-computer interaction research. Increasingly hosts learning engineering research at the intersection of AI, educational technology, and human-centered design — including significant work on AI-augmented teachers, fairness in educational AI, and learning dashboard design.

name: The Job of a College President
description: The founding document of learning engineering. Simon coined the term 'learning engineer' and argued that teaching effectiveness is a domain of expertise distinct from subject-matter knowledge, requiring its own scientific foundation in cognitive science. The philosophical and historical origin of everything in the field.

name: Online Education: A Catalyst for Higher Education Reforms
description: Re-introduced 'learning engineer' to higher education discourse at the moment MOOCs reached 58M+ global students. Recommended universities create dedicated learning engineering roles. Widely cited as the document that triggered the modern LE movement in higher education institutions.

name: High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering
description: Synthesized a 2020 convening of 100+ academics, policymakers, and practitioners to define ten strategic priorities in three domains: Better LE Infrastructure, Supporting Human Processes, and Better Learning Technologies. The field's most comprehensive contemporary roadmap and primary policy document framing LE's current agenda.

name: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Teaching and Learning: Insights and Recommendations
description: First major federal policy document addressing AI in education with explicit civil-rights framing. Documented algorithmic bias in e-proctoring systems and mandated algorithmic fairness and data representation requirements. Sets the ethical and legal floor for LE practice in the US.

name: EDUCAUSE Horizon Report: Teaching and Learning Edition
description: The annual predictive literature for edtech adoption across global higher education. Tracks short-, mid-, and long-term adoption horizons. Documents the gradual mainstreaming of LE concepts (analytics, adaptive systems, AI tutors) from forecast to widespread adoption across the 2017–2024 arc.

name: NMC Horizon Report: 2017 Higher Education Edition
description: Identified adaptive learning technologies and data-driven personalization as short-term adoption trends in higher education. Forecast the long-term restructuring of degrees around competency measurement. Part of the annual Horizon series that documented LE's gradual integration into mainstream institutional practice.

name: Learning Engineering Toolkit: Evidence-Based Practices from the Learning Sciences, Instructional Design, and Beyond
description: The field's primary practitioner handbook. Frames LE as a verb — an iterative, evidence-based problem-solving process — not a technology set or a job title. Covers lean-agile methods, HCI design, data instrumentation, motivation modeling, and predictive analytics. Technology-agnostic: principles apply equally to AI-driven software and low-tech interventions.

name: Learning Engineering for Online Education: Theoretical Contexts and Design-Based Examples
description: First book-length treatment of learning engineering applied to digital and distributed contexts. Saxberg's formulation — LE as applying learning science at 'massive, affordable, data-rich scale' — became widely adopted. Documents how online architectures enable continuous telemetry that peer-reviewed journals document years later.

name: Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model (LEAMM)
description: Organizational capability matrix allowing enterprises to assess and advance their LE practice across human-centered design, learning science integration, and data-driven iteration dimensions. Provides a structured pathway from ad-hoc instructional design toward mature learning engineering practice. The field's primary organizational self-assessment tool.

name: OpenSimon Toolkit
description: Flagship open infrastructure for LE research globally. Comprises DataShop, LearnSphere, CTAT, OLI, DiscourseDB, LightSide, Bazaar, Mathtutor, ChemCollective, and Echo. Enables researchers worldwide to build, test, and study LE systems without proprietary infrastructure.

name: 7 Things You Should Know About Learning Engineering
description: Widely circulated practitioner-oriented brief in the canonical EDUCAUSE '7 Things' series, introduced LE to a broad institutional technology audience including CIOs, provosts, and instructional technology directors. Accelerated LE vocabulary adoption in non-research higher education contexts.

name: Learning Engineering (IEEE Press)
description: The canonical textbook of the learning engineering field. Two-part structure covering LE foundations and practice. End notes are a primary seed source for corpus expansion — over 200 references spanning cognitive science, instructional design, analytics, and systems engineering. IEEE Press, 2022. Required reading for any LE practitioner or researcher.

name: IEEE ICICLE Resources Page
description: Curated resource list maintained by the IEEE ICICLE community. Includes papers, tools, programs, and community resources. The most authoritative practitioner-curated collection of LE resources, updated continuously as the field evolves.

name: How People Learn: Brain, Mind, Experience, and School (Expanded Edition)
description: The National Academies synthesis of cognitive science, neuroscience, and educational research that established how people learn. Free online. Widely cited as the most authoritative accessible synthesis of learning science. Essential T01 foundation layer and often the starting point for practitioners new to learning engineering.

name: The Forgotten Role of the Learning Engineer
description: Canonical entry-point essay calling for the learning engineering profession. Argued that education is the 'last great unapplied science' and that learning engineers — practitioners who apply learning science rigorously to educational products — are urgently needed at scale. Widely cited; often assigned as introductory reading for LE courses and programs.

name: ICICLE Body of Knowledge (BoK) — current draft
description: The field's emerging canonical definition document. Defines competency areas, vocabulary, and practice standards for learning engineering. Provides the official LEBOK framework that this corpus is organized to support. Version tracking required — update when new versions released.

name: MIL-STD-1472H: Human Engineering Design Criteria for Military Systems
description: DoD standard specifying human engineering design criteria for military systems, equipment, and facilities. The authoritative source for human factors requirements in defense training systems. Directly applicable to LE practitioners designing or procuring training systems in military and government contexts.

name: NASA Human Systems Integration Practitioner's Guide
description: NASA's operational guide to implementing HSI across the systems engineering lifecycle. Demonstrates how human-centered analysis (task analysis, workload, error analysis) integrates with traditional SE milestones. Applicable to complex LE system development in high-consequence environments.

name: INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook: A Guide for System Life Cycle Processes and Activities (4th ed.)
description: The authoritative practitioner reference for systems engineering processes and lifecycle management. Provides the SE process framework (requirements, architecture, integration, verification, validation) that learning engineering adapts for learning system development. Essential for LE practitioners with systems engineering backgrounds.

name: DoD Instruction 1322.26: Development, Management, and Delivery of Distributed Learning
description: DoD policy governing the design, management, and delivery of distributed (technology-based) learning for military training. Mandates ADDIE-based instructional systems design, learning objective taxonomies, and evaluation standards. The governance framework for LE deployment across the largest single training system in the world.

name: State of the Industry Report
description: Annual survey of corporate training investment, delivery modalities, and effectiveness measurement across hundreds of organizations. Provides LE practitioners with benchmarks for training hours, costs, technology adoption, and evaluation maturity. The most widely cited industry data on the state of organizational learning and development.

name: AC 120-51E: Crew Resource Management Training
description: FAA advisory circular defining standards for Crew Resource Management (CRM) training programs. CRM training is one of the most rigorous evidence-based training programs ever deployed at scale — a landmark case study in applying learning science to high-stakes professional training. Directly relevant to LE practitioners in aviation, healthcare simulation, and safety-critical training domains.

name: What Works Clearinghouse Handbook (v5.0)
description: The authoritative evidence standards framework for evaluating education research. Specifies the criteria an intervention study must meet to be considered high-quality evidence (randomized design, statistical power, attrition standards). Sets the evidentiary bar that LE evaluation must meet to influence policy and procurement decisions.

name: Human Systems Integration in the System Development Process: A New Look
description: National Academies report synthesizing research on integrating human factors into the system development process. Provides the HSI framework (human factors engineering, manpower, personnel, training, safety) that LE borrows when treating learning as a human-system performance problem. Foundational for LE practitioners working in defense and complex system contexts.

name: Siemens Pathway to Learning Engineering
description: Cloud-based, project-based STEM curriculum and educator professional development introducing engineering design and industry tools for secondary classrooms. Industry-sponsored LE pathway demonstrating how corporate partnerships can fund learning engineering curriculum at scale. Complements university LE pathways.

name: ADDIE Model — Branson et al. (1975) Interservice Procedures for Instructional Systems Development
description: Adjacent-field resource carried forward from landscape curation; enrichment pending.

name: Learning Engineering for Online Education (Dede, Richards, Saxberg eds.)
description: Edited volume by Dede, Richards, and Saxberg — the primary book-length treatment of learning engineering applied to online education. Covers LE process, adaptive systems, evidence, and scale.

name: Learning Engineering Process Description & Diagram (ICICLE)
description: Official ICICLE description of the Learning Engineering process, including the iterative cycle diagram showing problem framing, design, measurement, and improvement steps.

name: Learning Engineering Case Guide 1.0 (ICICLE)
description: Google Slides deck walking through real LE case examples. Shows how the LE process is applied in practice: problem formulation, data collection, evidence-based iteration.

name: Generalizable Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model
description: PDF defining a maturity model for organizations adopting LE practices. Stages describe progression from ad hoc instructional design through fully evidence-based, data-driven learning improvement.

name: Learning Engineering Implementation Checklist (ICICLE)
description: Google Doc checklist for practitioners implementing a learning engineering project. Covers project phases from needs analysis through evaluation and dissemination.

name: Examples of Roles & Expertise on an LE Team (ICICLE)
description: Google Doc describing the multidisciplinary roles typically needed on a learning engineering team: learning scientists, data analysts, instructional designers, engineers, subject matter experts.

name: Learning Design Principles Toolkit (McEldoon, ICICLE)
description: Google Drive toolkit by Katherine McEldoon connecting cognitive science principles to practical learning design decisions. Bridges learning science theory and LE design practice.

name: Bror Saxberg — MIT Festival of Learning Keynote 2023 (YouTube)
description: Keynote address by Bror Saxberg at the 2023 MIT Festival of Learning. Covers the vision of learning engineering, the gap between learning science and practice, and the role of data-driven design.

name: QIP Learning Engineering Video Series (YouTube playlist)
description: YouTube playlist produced by Quality Information Partners featuring practitioners and researchers discussing learning engineering principles, case studies, and field development.

name: Invitation to Learning Engineering Webinar Series (ICICLE, YouTube)
description: YouTube series of ICICLE-hosted webinars introducing the learning engineering field to new practitioners, covering foundational concepts, tools, and community resources.

name: IEEE Learning Engineering Podcast
description: Official IEEE ICICLE podcast series featuring conversations with learning engineering researchers, practitioners, and field leaders. Available on PodServe and Spotify.

name: Silver Lining for Learning — Art & Science of Learning Engineering (podcast, 2023)
description: Episode of the Silver Lining for Learning podcast exploring learning engineering as both an art and a science — balancing rigorous measurement with creative instructional design.

name: Silver Lining for Learning — Human-Centered Methods in LE (podcast, 2023)
description: Episode of the Silver Lining for Learning podcast focused on human-centered design methods applied within the learning engineering process.

name: Baker, Boser & Snow (2022) — Learning Engineering: A View on Where the Field Is At
description: Open-access article surveying the state of the learning engineering field as of 2022. Reviews definitions, practice norms, evidence standards, and key open problems.

name: Craig et al. (2023) — Learning Engineering Perspectives for Educational Systems
description: Multi-author article framing how learning engineering perspectives can transform educational systems at scale. Addresses policy, infrastructure, and community dimensions.

name: Lee (2023) — Learning Sciences & Learning Engineering: Natural or Artificial Distinction?
description: Scholarly article examining the relationship between learning sciences and learning engineering. Argues for integration rather than separation, tracing epistemological differences and convergences.

name: Kolodner (2023) — Learning Engineering: What It Is, Why I'm Involved
description: Personal essay and scholarly reflection by Janet Kolodner on the nature of learning engineering, its roots in learning sciences, and her reasons for engagement with the field.

name: 7 Things You Should Know About Learning Engineering (EDUCAUSE, 2018)
description: Accessible EDUCAUSE brief introducing learning engineering to higher-education technologists and administrators. Covers definition, typical activities, prospects, and challenges.

name: High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering (Baker & Boser, 2021)
description: Report by Ryan Baker and Beth Boser identifying the highest-leverage opportunities for learning engineering investment. Covers evidence gaps, scale barriers, and field infrastructure needs.

name: The Learning Agency — EdSurge LE Series
description: Collection of practitioner-oriented articles from The Learning Agency published in EdSurge. Covers LE field development, practitioner perspectives, and connections to education reform.

name: AECT Learning Engagement Activated Podcast — Jim Goodell Episode (2022)
description: Podcast episode featuring Jim Goodell discussing learning engineering principles, the ICICLE community, and the future of evidence-based learning design in professional development contexts.

name: Goodell (2019) — Are You Doing Learning Engineering or Instructional Design?
description: Practitioner article by Jim Goodell drawing the distinction between instructional design and learning engineering. Emphasizes data use, iteration, and empirical grounding as key differentiators.

name: Learning Engineering at a Glance (Schatz, Goodell & Kessler, Army Press, 2023)
description: Concise overview of learning engineering for military and defense training contexts, published by US Army Press. Bridges LE field concepts with high-consequence domain workforce applications.

name: State of XR & Immersive Learning Report 2021 (iLRN / ICICLE)
description: Annual state-of-the-field report on extended reality (XR/VR/AR) in learning contexts, co-produced with ICICLE. Covers research landscape, practitioner adoption, and evidence base for immersive learning.

name: Data Use Throughout the Learning Engineering Process (ICICLE graphic)
description: Infographic and supporting documentation mapping how different types of data — formative, summative, behavioral, survey — are collected and used at each stage of the LE process.

name: Learning Engineering Adjacent Academic Programs Directory (ICICLE)
description: Google Doc directory of academic programs adjacent to or incorporating learning engineering, including LE-specific degrees, learning sciences programs, educational technology programs, and HCI programs.

name: Learning Engineering is a Team Sport (ICICLE presentation, 2024)
description: ICICLE presentation emphasizing the collaborative, multidisciplinary nature of learning engineering teams. Addresses how different domain experts contribute to LE projects and how to build effective teams.

name: Teaming Up to Improve Medical & Healthcare Education (Kurzweil & Marcellas, 2020)
description: Case study and practitioner article applying learning engineering team approaches to medical and healthcare professional education. Demonstrates LE in a high-stakes, complex domain.

name: Build a Learning Data Dream Team (Torrance, Lin & Goodell, ATD, 2024)
description: Practitioner article on assembling multidisciplinary teams to leverage learning data effectively. Covers roles (data analyst, learning engineer, instructional designer) and collaborative workflows.

name: IDEO Design Thinking Process (ideou.com)
description: IDEO's publicly available overview of the human-centered design thinking process. Referenced in LE as a precursor and complement to the iterative LE process, particularly for empathy and problem framing stages.

name: Van Campenhout et al. (2023) — Student-Centered Educational Data Science Through Learning Engineering
description: Research article integrating educational data science methods within a learning engineering framework. Demonstrates how student-centered analysis and iterative design reinforce each other in practice.

name: Herb Simon (1967) — The Job of a College President
description: Archival essay by Herbert A. Simon — foundational thinker behind cognitive science and bounded rationality — on university leadership and the role of evidence in educational decision-making. Cited as a historical intellectual ancestor of learning engineering.

name: Advancing Workforce Learning through Learning Engineering
description: On October 23rd, 2025, the ICICLE Workforce Market Interest Group (MIG) led a webinar on Advancing Workforce Learning through Learning Engineering. The MIG co-chairs, DJ Jaeger and John Costa, explored how learning engineering supports workforce transformation through data-informed design, system-level thinking, and evidence-based practices that link learning to performance outcomes.

name: AECT Learner Engagement Activated — Jim Goodell episode
description: Fensie interviews Goodell in a long-form format — origin story, ICICLE's founding, and the case for LE as an engineering discipline. If you want biographical context on one of the field's anchors, start here.

name: All Rise the Learning Engineers
description: An early (2018) public conversation about the LE role. Useful as a time-capsule: what the field thought it was before ICICLE consolidated. Barr and Robson are both early movers; the episode holds up.

name: Applying Learning Engineering in a Global Context
description: An Invitation of Learning Engineering held on February 25, 2026, explored how learning engineering is adapted globally in a local context. Hosted by the Globalisation Special Interest Group, Carolina Ali Fojaco, Chief Learning Officer at Project Alianza, provided a deep dive on how they used learning engineering to iterate and scale AnGo, an offline, AI-assisted literacy app to help children in Central America build foundational reading skills.

name: Applying Learning Engineering in Mission-Critical Environments
description: The IEEE ICICLE Government and Military Market Interest Group (MIG) led by MIG co-chairs, Eric Ultes and Jason Virtue, held on July 18th, 2025, explored how learning engineering can address learning transfer challenges in mission-critical settings and support evidence-informed training, innovation, and performance through a case study featuring Kevin Owens from the Applied Research Laboratories at the University of Texas, a U.S. Department of Defense University Affiliated Research Center (UARC).

name: Crazy Idea: What If We Use Engineering to Develop and Deliver Engineering Education?
description: Goodell in conversation with Azad (IFEES) on the provocative inverse of our normal framing: if LE is how we rigorously teach people to engineer, why don't we use LE to teach engineering itself? A 2025 entry worth watching for the international-engineering-education perspective.

name: Discover the World of Learning Engineering in Post-Secondary Contexts
description: The Invitation to Learning Engineering held on March 26th, 2025, explored the application of learning engineering across post-secondary contexts. This session highlighted use cases from ICICLE Higher Ed Market Interest Group members, Marilu Lam-Herrera from CPA Western School of Business and Lauren Totino and Aaron Kessler from MIT.

name: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2019
description: The inaugural ICICLE conference -- the opening public record of the learning engineering community's annual meeting and the baseline for how the field has evolved since.

name: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2022
description: 2022 edition of the ICICLE annual conference. Program, proceedings, and recorded sessions available.

name: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2023
description: 2023 edition of the ICICLE annual conference. Program, proceedings, and recorded sessions available.

name: ICICLE Conference on Learning Engineering 2024
description: The field's flagship annual gathering. The 2024 ICICLE conference is the most recent edition, with proceedings, recorded sessions, and the latest practitioner and research presentations.

name: Learning Engineering: A path to empowering learners in and for the Age of AI — Panel
description: Panel response to the McNamara and Garg keynotes above. Worth watching as a trio — the panel is where the rough edges of AI-in-LE show up (evaluation, equity, disciplinary identity).

name: Learning Engineering in the Age of AI — Kumar Garg
description: Garg (Walton-funded LE efforts; formerly OSTP) frames AI-and-LE as an evidence and infrastructure problem, not a pedagogical one. The pitch: the field needs many more studies, faster, to match AI's pace — and LE is the discipline set up to do that.

name: Learning Engineering: What We Know, What We Can Do — Bror Saxberg keynote
description: Saxberg's clearest public statement of the learning-engineer role: why instructional design's compliance model falls short at scale, what evidence-based iteration actually looks like in industry, and where the next generation of LEs needs to come from. The canonical 'what is LE' talk for newcomers.

name: Leveraging AI and Learning Engineering in Large-Scale Learning Sciences — Danielle McNamara
description: McNamara's case for AI-enabled learning engineering at scale — grounded in her two decades of NLP-and-learning-sciences work. Argues that the most promising AI applications in LE aren't content generation but formative measurement and adaptive scaffolding. Technical but accessible.

name: Silver Lining for Learning — Applying Human-Centered Engineering Methods to Learning
description: Kolodner, Goodell, and Schatz on where human-centered design methods actually fit inside the LE process — which ones matter, which get applied lazily, and where the practitioner discipline still has gaps.

name: Silver Lining for Learning — The Art and Science of Learning Engineering
description: Roscoe, Craig, and McNamara on what makes LE distinct from adjacent fields — and where art/intuition still lives inside what's meant to be an engineering discipline. A more philosophical companion to the ICICLE 2024 materials.

name: Unlock the Learning Engineer in You — pK-12 Context
description: The Invitation to Learning Engineering webinar held on December 11th, 2024, highlighted a use case from Carnegie Learning on the application of learning engineering in pK-12 context. The case study featured Steve Ritter (Founder & Chief Scientist), Husni Almoubayyed (AI Engineering Manager), and Rae Bastoni (Senior UX Researcher). They showcased insights from the work on facilitating math learning with adaptive reading supports.

name: Examples of Roles and Areas of Expertise on a LE Team
2020 EDUCAUSE Horizon Report — Teaching and Learning Edition
Bridging learning research and teaching practice for the public good: The learning engineer
Data-Informed Course Improvement: The Application of Learning Engineering in the Classroom
description: Higher-ed classroom case study. Fensie walks through a specific course intervention, what data moved, and what didn't — an honest practitioner paper at a major learning sciences venue.

name: Designing a Student Progress Panel for Formative Practice: A Learning Engineering Process
description: A compact case study of the LE process applied to a real adaptive-learning product (Acrobatiq). Worth reading back-to-back with Fensie's ICLS piece on Data-Informed Course Improvement.

name: Designing for Transfer: Developing a Skill-Based Simulation Using Learning Engineering Design Frameworks
description: MODSIM paper on building a skills simulator with transfer as the explicit design target. Good example of LE-in-defense-adjacent training contexts where simulators are the primary medium.

name: LE Toolkit — Learning Engineering Applies the Learning Sciences (open access chapter)
LE Toolkit — Learning Engineering is a Process (open access chapter)
Learning Engineering Enlightenment: Think Like an Engineer
description: Wagner's 2024 follow-on to her long thread of LE-vs-ID articles. The sharpest version of her argument that the shift isn't tools, it's disposition: LEs reason like engineers about uncertainty and evidence.

name: The Science of Remote Learning
description: Pandemic-era compilation from MIT Open Learning distilling what the learning sciences say about effective remote instruction. Practical, evidence-based, and widely shared during the 2020 pivot.

name: Teaching Online Preparation Toolkit (TOPkit)
description: UCF's open toolkit for preparing faculty to teach online — widely referenced as a pragmatic, institutionally-scaled resource. ICICLE points to it from their resources page; it's one of the few real-world implementations of LE-style staff development at scale.

name: Munroe proposes applying engineering principles to educational efficiency
description: One of the earliest documented calls for treating the design of educational systems as an engineering discipline, predating the formal field by half a century.

name: W.W. Charters asks 'Is There a Field of Educational Engineering?' at Ohio State University
description: First substantive academic debate over whether curriculum design and instruction could constitute a formal engineering discipline. Established the core question the field would spend decades answering.

name: Bloom's Taxonomy of Educational Objectives published
description: Provided the first widely adopted framework for classifying learning objectives hierarchically. Became the dominant vocabulary for instructional design and later for competency frameworks used in learning engineering systems.

name: Herbert Simon coins the term 'learning engineer' in 'The Job of a College President'
description: The founding document of the field. Simon argued that content expertise does not equal expertise in facilitating learning, and proposed dedicated professionals — learning engineers — to design evidence-based learning experiences. Published in Educational Record, 48(1), 68–78.

name: SOAR cognitive architecture becomes operational (Newell, Laird, Rosenbloom at CMU)
description: SOAR modeled human problem-solving and learning through 'chunking' — a key mechanism for understanding how procedural knowledge is acquired. Foundational for cognitive modeling in intelligent tutoring systems.

name: LISP Tutor deployed at Carnegie Mellon — first empirically validated intelligent tutoring system
description: Demonstrated that a computer tutor based on cognitive models (ACT theory) could produce measurable learning gains. Proof of concept for the entire ITS paradigm and direct antecedent of Cognitive Tutor.

name: ACT-R (Adaptive Control of Thought—Rational) theory formalized by John Anderson
description: ACT-R provided a unified cognitive architecture distinguishing declarative from procedural memory and specifying how practice drives skill acquisition. Became the theoretical backbone for Cognitive Tutors and much of the ITS field.

name: First International Conference on Intelligent Tutoring Systems held in Montreal
description: Established the formal academic community for ITS research. The ITS conference series (biennial) remains the premier venue for computational learning engineering research to this day.

name: John Sweller publishes foundational paper on Cognitive Load Theory
description: Cognitive Load Theory formalized the constraints working memory imposes on learning and provided empirically grounded design rules for instructional materials. One of the most-cited theoretical frameworks in instructional design and learning engineering.

name: Koedinger & Anderson map cognitive expertise into computational production rules (Cognitive Science)
description: Bridged cognitive psychology and AI by showing how expert geometry knowledge could be encoded as production rules usable in an ITS. Established the methodology for building the first generation of Cognitive Tutors.

name: Ann Brown publishes 'Design Experiments' in Journal of the Learning Sciences
description: Established design-based research as the methodological framework for testing engineered learning interventions in authentic classroom settings. Over 2,800 citations; most influential paper in JLS history. Became the basis for how learning engineers validate systems in the wild.

name: Scardamalia & Bereiter publish 'Computer Support for Knowledge-Building Communities' in JLS
description: Engineered the socio-cognitive framework for collaborative digital learning platforms. With over 1,800 citations, this paper shaped how online and CSCL systems are designed to support collective knowledge construction rather than individual acquisition.

name: Corbett, Koedinger & Anderson publish Bayesian Knowledge Tracing architecture in HCI Handbook
description: Operationalized real-time student modeling: BKT uses a hidden Markov model to track the probability that a student has learned each knowledge component. Became the dominant algorithm in ITS student models and is still widely used.

name: Herbert Simon dies; CMU institutionalizes his legacy through the Simon Initiative
description: Simon's death prompted CMU to formally organize the Simon Initiative, creating an ecosystem around learning engineering that includes LearnLab, OLI, DataShop, and the METALS master's program.

name: Baker & Yacef codify Educational Data Mining as a discipline (JEDM)
description: Distinguished EDM from learning analytics and positioned it as a distinct scientific field with its own methods, journals, and conferences. Over 1,500 citations. Catalyzed the formation of the International Educational Data Mining Society and the JEDM journal.

name: First Learning Analytics and Knowledge (LAK) conference; Society for Learning Analytics Research (SoLAR) founded
description: Formalized learning analytics as a distinct discipline alongside EDM. LAK emphasizes practice-oriented analytics and ethical deployment; SoLAR anchors the community. LA and EDM now constitute two of the three major computational LE conference series.

name: MIT OEPI publishes 'Online Education: A Catalyst for Higher Education Reforms'
description: Willcox, Sarma & Lippel re-injected 'learning engineer' into mainstream higher education discourse at the moment MOOCs were serving 58M+ students globally. Directly recommended universities create learning engineering roles. Catalyzed institutional adoption of the LE framing.

name: IEEE ICICLE (Industry Consortium on Learning Engineering) chartered under IEEE LTSC
description: First formal standards-aligned professional body specifically for learning engineering. ICICLE produces maturity models, competency frameworks, annual proceedings, and the Journal of Learning Engineering — anchoring the field's professional infrastructure.

name: Learning Engineering for Online Education published (Dede, Richards, Saxberg, eds.)
description: First book-length treatment of learning engineering applied to digital and distributed contexts. Saxberg's framing of LE as applying learning science 'at massive scale' became widely adopted. Routledge.

name: 'High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering' report published (Baker, Boser, Shelley)
description: Drew on a 2020 convening of 100+ academics and practitioners to define ten strategic priorities across 'Better LE Infrastructure,' 'Supporting Human Processes,' and 'Better Learning Technologies.' One of the most cited contemporary LE policy documents.

name: U.S. Dept. of Education releases 'Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Teaching and Learning'
description: First major federal policy document addressing AI in education with explicit civil-rights framing. Documented algorithmic bias in e-proctoring, mandated fairness requirements. Set policy guardrails that now frame responsible learning engineering practice.

name: Learning Engineering Toolkit published (Goodell & Kolodner, eds., Taylor & Francis)
description: First practitioner-facing textbook defining LE as a verb — an iterative engineering process — rather than a role or technology set. Covers lean-agile methods, data instrumentation, motivation modeling, and predictive analytics. Agnostic to technology stack.

name: Journal of the Learning Sciences
description:
Founded 1991; the multidisciplinary anchor journal for theoretical models, epistemology, and design-based research in the learning sciences. Home of Ann Brown's 'Design Experiments' (1992, 2,800+ citations) and Scardamalia & Bereiter's knowledge-building paper (1994). Sets the methodological standard for design-based research in education.
Publishes the socio-cognitive and humanistic tradition within learning engineering — essential for grounding algorithmic approaches in rich theories of how people learn in context. JLS is the venue where learning engineering practitioners find the theoretical warrant for their design decisions and the strongest empirical challenges to purely technocentric approaches.

name: Journal of Educational Data Mining (JEDM)
description:
Open-access journal dedicated to algorithms and methodologies for analyzing educational data: knowledge tracing, automated feature engineering, predictive student modeling, and behavioral analysis. Baker & Yacef's founding codification of the EDM field (2009) was published here, establishing the taxonomy of methods that still organizes the community.
Premier venue for computational learning engineering methods. Diamond open access — no fees for authors or readers. For LE practitioners developing or evaluating data-driven components, JEDM provides the most rigorous algorithmic benchmarks and the broadest survey of available methods.

name: IEEE Transactions on Learning Technologies
description:
Covers technical design and architecture of learning systems: adaptive algorithms, NLP conversational agents, multimodal telemetry (eye tracking, EEG, galvanic skin response), and large-scale platform engineering. Requires substantive technical innovation in computer science alongside pedagogical validity — not purely educational research and not purely CS research.
Key venue for AI-in-education engineering papers. IEEE TLT is where learning engineering practitioners find the most rigorous technical treatments of system architecture, with enough pedagogical grounding to distinguish educational effectiveness from mere technological novelty.

name: Journal of Learning Analytics (JLA)
description:
Official publication of SoLAR. Covers the socio-technical implications of educational data: dashboard design, algorithmic ethics, data privacy, human-in-the-loop architectures, and equity in analytics deployment. Publishes empirical evaluations of analytics interventions alongside conceptual and critical scholarship.
Complements JEDM's algorithmic focus with practice implications and ethical framing. For LE practitioners deploying analytics in institutional contexts, JLA provides the strongest guidance on implementation, adoption, and the institutional and ethical conditions under which analytics actually improves learning outcomes.

name: International Journal of Artificial Intelligence in Education (IJAIED)
description:
Premier venue for intelligent tutoring systems, generative AI in education, deep cognitive modeling, and rigorous evaluation of AI versus human tutoring. Covers algorithmic accuracy evaluations, human factors studies, and pedagogical agent design. The home journal for rigorous AIED research between conference cycles.
IJAIED publishes extended versions of AIED conference papers and independent journal submissions, providing the most comprehensive coverage of the theory-to-system pipeline in AI-enhanced learning. For LE practitioners working with LLMs, knowledge tracers, or pedagogical agents, IJAIED is the primary source for validated system architectures and evaluation frameworks.

name: Journal of Learning Engineering (JoLE)
description:
The only journal specifically dedicated to learning engineering as a named field. Diamond Open Access — no article processing charges for authors or readers. Community-driven by ICICLE; provides the 'Learning Engineering Primer' to align multidisciplinary terminology across contributing disciplines.
JoLE is the primary publication venue for work that explicitly frames itself within the LE paradigm — engineering rigor applied to learning system design, measurement, and iteration. Because the journal is young and the field is emerging, it requires venue-based discovery rather than citation-network search; curators and practitioners should monitor it directly rather than relying on index coverage.

name: International Journal of STEM Education
description:
Discipline-based education research (DBER) covering problem-based learning, flipped classrooms, educational robotics, and STEM learning outcomes at scale. Published by Springer Open. Bridges disciplinary content expertise with empirical learning research, providing evidence for domain-specific instructional strategies in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.
Critical venue for LE researchers designing domain-specific systems in STEM contexts. IJSTE papers provide the empirical base for content-specific design decisions — what works in undergraduate physics is not always what works in K-12 coding education — and offer validated instruments and outcome measures that LE practitioners can adapt.

name: Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making
description: Adjacent-field journal carried forward from landscape curation; enrichment pending.

name: Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society
description: Adjacent-field journal carried forward from landscape curation; enrichment pending.

name: IEEE ICICLE — Industry Consortium on Learning Engineering
description:
Professional standards body for learning engineering. Produces the LE Adoption Maturity Model, competency frameworks, annual conference proceedings, and the Journal of Learning Engineering. Chartered 2017 under IEEE LTSC.
The institutional anchor of the modern LE field, providing the primary community infrastructure, shared vocabulary, and standards work that distinguish learning engineering as a professional discipline.

name: The Simon Initiative
description:
Cross-disciplinary learning engineering ecosystem at CMU named for Herbert Simon. Encompasses LearnLab, OLI, DataShop/LearnSphere, METALS master's program, and the OpenSimon Toolkit. Hundreds of affiliated faculty collaborate across schools to design, instrument, and iteratively improve learning systems using rigorous data.
The world's most integrated LE research-to-practice ecosystem, housing the world's largest open educational log data bank and serving as the primary institutional model for what learning engineering looks like at institutional scale.

name: Society for Learning Analytics Research (SoLAR)
description:
International academic society promoting learning analytics research, practice, and policy. Organizes the annual LAK (Learning Analytics and Knowledge) conference and the Learning Analytics Summer Institute (LASI). Publishes the Journal of Learning Analytics. Founded 2011 alongside the first LAK conference.
Emphasizes practice-oriented, ethical analytics distinct from pure EDM, with strong attention to equity, transparency, and the socio-technical implications of deploying analytics in educational contexts.

name: Penn Center for Learning Analytics
description:
Research center at the University of Pennsylvania directing educational data mining and learning engineering policy work, led by Ryan Baker. Produced the influential 'High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering' report (2021), which mapped priority areas for the field.
Baker serves as editor of the Journal of Educational Data Mining and is a primary organizer of the EDM conference. The center bridges computational EDM methods with applied learning engineering, making it a key node in the national LE policy conversation.

name: HCII — Human-Computer Interaction Institute, Carnegie Mellon
description:
Academic home of learning sciences and technologies at CMU. Houses Ken Koedinger, Al Corbett, and the PACT Center. Offers PhD and MS programs integrating HCI, learning sciences, and data science. Co-originator of learning engineering as a named field.
Home of DataShop, CTAT (Cognitive Tutor Authoring Tools), and the Cognitive Tutor research lineage — among the most replicated bodies of evidence in applied learning science. The HCII is the primary academic source of the methodological and theoretical frameworks that define learning engineering as a discipline.

name: International Educational Data Mining Society (IEDMS)
description:
Society organizing the annual EDM (Educational Data Mining) conference and publishing the Journal of Educational Data Mining. Focuses on computational, discovery-oriented analysis of educational data, with emphasis on mathematical and algorithmic approaches to knowledge tracing, student modeling, and behavioral prediction.
EDM is complementary to but distinct from learning analytics: EDM prioritizes algorithmic rigor and discovery, while LA emphasizes socio-technical implications and practice. Both communities are essential to the learning engineering toolkit.

name: Learning Sciences Research Institute, UIC
description:
Interdisciplinary research institute at the University of Illinois Chicago spanning learning sciences, computational methods, and educational equity. Conducts research that bridges theoretical learning science and applied educational intervention, with particular attention to equity and access in urban educational contexts.
LSRI provides a model for LE research that integrates rigorous methodology with a commitment to socially situated, equity-centered approaches — an important counterweight to purely technocentric conceptions of the field.

name: IEEE ICICLE SIGs & MIGs
description:
Official listing of IEEE ICICLE Special Interest Groups (SIGs) and Market Interest Groups (MIGs), including chairs, meeting cadence, and subscription information. Active groups span: Competencies/Credentials/Curriculum SIG, Design for Learning SIG, Tools SIG, Higher Education MIG, Government/Military MIG, pK-12 MIG, Workforce MIG, and Globalisation/Localisation SIG.
Open participation — the primary roster of active community leaders in learning engineering practice. SIGs and MIGs are the working layer where ICICLE standards, frameworks, and white papers are actually drafted; joining a SIG is the most direct path to participating in the field's institutional development.

name: LIVE Learning Innovation Incubator — Vanderbilt Peabody
description:
Peabody College incubator supporting interdisciplinary learning innovation at Vanderbilt University. Brings together researchers, designers, and technologists to develop and evaluate novel learning systems, with emphasis on evidence-based design and emerging AI applications.
Vanderbilt LIVE teams participate in national AI-for-learning efforts, including AI-ALOE partner work on intelligent text and workplace learning tools. The incubator model — rapid prototyping with embedded evaluation — exemplifies the learning engineering design cycle applied at a research university.

name: National AI Institute for Adult Learning and Online Education (AI-ALOE)
description:
NSF-funded National AI Institute headquartered at Georgia Tech, developing AI theories and systems for adult online learning, intelligent tutors, and workforce upskilling. Consortium includes Georgia Tech, Vanderbilt, Harvard, and other partners. Pursues both foundational AI research and applied system development for real-world adult learners.
Primary Georgia Tech anchor for AI×learning engineering at scale. AI-ALOE represents one of the largest federal investments in AI-driven learning engineering, with particular focus on underserved adult learners and the intersection of workforce development with adaptive educational technology.

name: International Society for the Learning Sciences (ISLS)
description:
Academic society organizing the annual ICLS (International Conference of the Learning Sciences) and CSCL (Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning) conferences. Bridges learning science theory and instructional design practice. Emphasizes socio-cultural and humanistic perspectives alongside computational approaches.
Essential for grounding algorithmic LE systems in rich theories of how people learn in social and institutional contexts. ISLS conferences are where learning scientists and education researchers most directly interrogate the assumptions embedded in AI and data-driven learning systems.

name: Human Factors and Ergonomics Society (HFES)
description:
Professional society for human factors and ergonomics. Publishes the Human Factors journal and organizes the annual HFES conference. The Training Systems technical group directly addresses LE-relevant human factors in instructional systems design, including workload, situation awareness, and performance measurement in training environments.
Key community for practitioners designing learning systems in complex, safety-critical environments — military, aviation, nuclear, and healthcare. Human factors methods (task analysis, cognitive workload assessment, human-in-the-loop testing) are essential LE tools when learning outcomes must be validated against real operational performance.

name: INCOSE Systems Engineering Body of Knowledge (SEBoK)
description:
Open wiki-based body of knowledge for systems engineering, maintained by INCOSE. Includes the Human Systems Integration knowledge area. Covers SE process frameworks: requirements engineering, architecture, integration, verification and validation, and lifecycle management.
The SE process framework that SEBoK codifies is the engineering process backbone that learning engineering adapts for learning system development. Understanding SE fundamentals — especially requirements traceability and V&V — is essential for LE practitioners working at the boundary of instructional design and systems engineering.

name: Naturalistic Decision Making Association (NDM)
description:
Research community studying expert decision-making under realistic, complex conditions. NDM methods — critical decision method (CDM), cognitive task analysis (CTA), recognition-primed decision modeling — are the primary toolkit for eliciting tacit expert knowledge in high-stakes training domains.
Key source of needs analysis methods for learning engineering in professional training contexts: military, healthcare, aviation, firefighting, and emergency management. When LE practitioners must translate expert performance into instructional objectives, NDM methods provide the validated approach for surfacing knowledge that experts cannot articulate unprompted.

name: Credential Engine / CTDL
description:
Open-source data standard and registry for describing credentials, competencies, and learning pathways. The Credential Transparency Description Language (CTDL) encodes 1,200+ properties for representing what people know and can do, enabling machine-readable linkages between learning experiences, competencies, and credentials across providers and platforms.
The emerging national infrastructure for credential-level knowledge representation in competency-based learning engineering systems. For LE practitioners designing workforce-aligned learning systems, CTDL provides the shared ontology needed to map learning outcomes to job requirements and to stack credentials into coherent pathways at scale.

name: LIVE Learning Innovation Incubator (Vanderbilt Peabody)
description: Peabody incubator supporting interdisciplinary learning innovation; participates in national AI-for-learning efforts.

name: ISLS Annual Meeting
description: Annual conference of the International Society of the Learning Sciences, encompassing ICLS and CSCL communities.

name: INCOSE — International Council on Systems Engineering
description: Adjacent-field resource carried forward from landscape curation; enrichment pending.

name: Credential Engine / CTDL (Credential Transparency Description Language)
description: Adjacent-field resource carried forward from landscape curation; enrichment pending.

name: Applied Research Laboratories (ARL) — University of Texas at Austin
description: Adjacent-field resource carried forward from landscape curation; enrichment pending.

name: OpenSimon Community (CMU Simon Initiative)
description: Open-access community and toolkit collection from CMU's Simon Initiative. Hosts LearnSphere DataShop, OLI course materials, and research infrastructure for learning engineering at scale.

name: MIT LEAP Group — Learning Engineering and Practice
description: MIT research group applying learning engineering principles to university-level STEM education. Produces open courseware, edX materials, and research on evidence-based pedagogy at scale.

name: Playful Journey Lab / MITili (MIT)
description: MIT lab focused on game-based and playful learning engineering. MITili (MIT Integrated Learning Initiative) applies data-driven, iterative design to immersive and game-based educational experiences.

name: Advanced Distributed Learning Initiative (ADL)
description: DoD-funded learning-technology R&D center. Birthplace of SCORM and xAPI. Substantial overlap with the ICICLE Government/Military MIG; they co-sponsor iFEST each year.

name: IEEE Learning Technology Standards Committee (LTSC)
description: IEEE standards body responsible for learning-technology interoperability standards. The institutional home of xAPI-adjacent work and a natural partner to ICICLE's working groups.

name: MIT Integrated Learning Initiative (MITili)
description: MIT-wide initiative bridging learning science research and practice. Funds cross-disciplinary work on how people learn and publishes actionable guidance — a key academic node in the LE ecosystem.

name: Playful Journey Lab
description: MIT lab building playful assessment tools and game-based learning approaches. Runs the 'Schools of Tomorrow' Learning Engineering Project Blueprint — an example of LE applied to K-12 assessment innovation.

name: The Learning Agency
description: Non-profit research and policy shop led by Ulrich Boser, the co-author of 'High-Leverage Opportunities for LE.' Runs several open-data competitions and the Learning Engineering Google Group that ICICLE references.

name: Kenneth R. Koedinger
description: Co-originator of learning engineering as a field. Creator of Cognitive Tutor and Carnegie Learning, deployed in thousands of schools. Founded DataShop (now LearnSphere), the world's largest open repository of educational log data. Coined the modern formulation of learning engineering as an applied, data-driven discipline. Past chair of the EDM community and co-director of LearnLab at CMU.
role: Co-originator of learning engineering as a named field; Cognitive Tutor pioneer; DataShop founder

name: Ryan S. J. d. Baker
description: Pioneer in educational data mining and learning analytics. Directs the Penn Center for Learning Analytics. Co-author of High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering (2021), the field's most-cited contemporary policy document. Editor of the Journal of Educational Data Mining. Known for work on detecting gaming-the-system and off-task behavior in ITS.
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Graduate School of Education
role: EDM pioneer; director of Penn Center for Learning Analytics; high-leverage LE policy voice

name: Bror Saxberg
description: Early champion of learning engineering as a named field. Wrote influential 2016 Atlantic piece calling for a new 'learning engineer' profession. Led learning science at Chan Zuckerberg Initiative, framing LE as 'precision education' analogous to precision medicine. Previously Chief Learning Officer at Kaplan. ICICLE advisory board member and co-editor of Learning Engineering for Online Education (Routledge, 2019).
affiliation: Formerly Chan Zuckerberg Initiative / Kaplan; founder of LearningForge
role: Learning science practitioner; LE thought leader; industry-academic bridge

name: Jim Goodell
description: Co-founder of the Learning Engineering book (IEEE Press). Co-editor of the canonical Learning Engineering Toolkit (Taylor & Francis, 2023). Co-chaired IEEE ICICLE, building the professional infrastructure for learning engineering. Framed LE as a verb — a process — rather than a noun. Drove development of xAPI standards and competency frameworks for interoperable LE systems.
role: LE standards architect; Learning Engineering Toolkit co-editor; ICICLE community builder

name: John Behrens
description: Evidence-centered design (ECD) researcher and practitioner. Led large-scale assessment innovation at ETS and Pearson. Co-developed the ECD framework with Robert Mislevy, bridging psychometrics and instructional design. Active ICICLE contributor who defined the role of the learning engineer in assessment and credentialing contexts. Applies Bayesian inference networks to adaptive assessment design.
role: Psychometrician; evidence-centered design architect; learning analytics at scale

name: Jodi Lis
description: Co-chair of the Competencies, Credentials and Curriculum (CCC) SIG and the Globalisation/Localisation SIG within IEEE ICICLE. Develops practitioner competency frameworks for the learning engineering profession and works to adapt LE methods for international and multilingual contexts.
affiliation: IEEE ICICLE — CCC SIG & Globalisation/Localisation SIG
role: LE competency framework developer; ICICLE CCC and Globalisation/Localisation SIG co-chair

name: Bedriye Akson
description: Co-chair of the Competencies, Credentials and Curriculum SIG (IEEE ICICLE), focusing on curriculum alignment and credential frameworks for learning engineering practitioners. MIT-affiliated researcher bringing academic LE research into ICICLE's practitioner community.
role: Learning design researcher; ICICLE CCC SIG co-chair

name: Aaron Kessler
description: Co-chair of the Design for Learning SIG within IEEE ICICLE, advancing human-centered design (HCD) methods as core LE practice. MIT-affiliated, bringing HCD and systems thinking frameworks into the LE practitioner community.
affiliation: MIT; IEEE ICICLE — Design for Learning SIG
role: Human-centered design researcher; ICICLE Design for Learning SIG co-chair

name: Amy Parent
description: Co-chair of the Design for Learning SIG (IEEE ICICLE). Industry practitioner from Salience Learning, bridging corporate L&D and academic HCD frameworks within the learning engineering field.
affiliation: Salience Learning; IEEE ICICLE — Design for Learning SIG
role: LE design practitioner; ICICLE Design for Learning SIG co-chair

name: Adesola Ogundimu
description: Co-chair of the Globalisation/Localisation SIG within IEEE ICICLE, adapting learning engineering frameworks for international, multilingual, and culturally diverse contexts. Johns Hopkins affiliation connects global health education and equity perspectives to the LE field.
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University; IEEE ICICLE — Globalisation/Localisation SIG
role: LE globalisation researcher; ICICLE Globalisation/Localisation SIG co-chair

name: Mei-Mei Li
description: Co-chair of the Students and Grads SIG within IEEE ICICLE, supporting mentorship and career development for students pursuing learning engineering careers. ASU affiliation connects the Learning Engineering Institute with ICICLE's professional community.
affiliation: Arizona State University; IEEE ICICLE — Students and Grads SIG
role: Emerging LE professional; ICICLE Students and Grads SIG co-chair

name: Abby Bregman
description: Co-chair of the Students and Grads SIG within IEEE ICICLE, supporting career development and mentorship for emerging learning engineering practitioners.
affiliation: IEEE ICICLE — Students and Grads SIG
role: Emerging LE professional; ICICLE Students and Grads SIG co-chair

name: Li (Lee) Liang
description: Co-chair of the Students and Grads SIG within IEEE ICICLE, supporting emerging learning engineering professionals through mentorship, community events, and career development resources.
affiliation: IEEE ICICLE — Students and Grads SIG
role: Emerging LE professional; ICICLE Students and Grads SIG co-chair

name: Erin Czerwinski
description: Co-chair of the Tools SIG within IEEE ICICLE, curating and evaluating learning engineering tools and technology stacks. CMU affiliation connects research-grade LE tools (OpenSimon, DataShop/LearnSphere) with ICICLE's practitioner community. Chaired the 2023 ICICLE Annual Meeting held at CMU.
affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University; IEEE ICICLE — Tools SIG
role: LE tools researcher; ICICLE Tools SIG co-chair; ICICLE 2023 Conference Chair

name: Baptiste Moreau-Pernet
description: Co-chair of the Tools SIG within IEEE ICICLE, evaluating and promoting tools that support the learning engineering workflow from design through measurement.
role: LE tools practitioner; ICICLE Tools SIG co-chair

name: Emily Marasco
description: Co-chair of the Higher Education MIG within IEEE ICICLE, applying LE methods to university teaching and curriculum design. University of Calgary researcher bridging engineering education pedagogy with learning engineering practice.
affiliation: University of Calgary; IEEE ICICLE — Higher Education MIG
role: Engineering education researcher; ICICLE Higher Education MIG co-chair

name: Rob Nyland
description: Co-chair of the Higher Education MIG within IEEE ICICLE, alongside Emily Marasco. Practitioner focus on applying LE methods within post-secondary instructional and faculty development contexts.
role: Higher education LE practitioner; ICICLE Higher Education MIG co-chair

name: Eric Ultes
description: Co-chair of the Government / Military MIG within IEEE ICICLE. Brings simulation and technology-enabled training expertise from Caemil USA to large-scale government and defense workforce development programs.
affiliation: Caemil USA; IEEE ICICLE — Government / Military MIG
role: Military training technologist; ICICLE Government/Military MIG co-chair

name: Jason Virtue
description: Co-chair of the Government / Military MIG within IEEE ICICLE. Canadian Armed Forces practitioner applying LE methods to military professional development and training system design.
affiliation: Canadian Armed Forces; IEEE ICICLE — Government / Military MIG
role: Military training practitioner; ICICLE Government/Military MIG co-chair

name: Zarka Ali
description: Co-chair of the pK-12 MIG within IEEE ICICLE, advancing learning engineering methods in preK–12 settings where implementation differs significantly from higher education and corporate contexts.
role: K-12 LE practitioner; ICICLE pK-12 MIG co-chair

name: John Ellis
description: Co-chair of the pK-12 MIG within IEEE ICICLE. Dartmouth College affiliation connects computing and technology resources to K-12 learning engineering applications.
affiliation: Dartmouth College; IEEE ICICLE — pK-12 MIG
role: K-12 LE researcher; ICICLE pK-12 MIG co-chair

name: John Costa
description: Co-chair of the Workforce MIG within IEEE ICICLE, advancing LE methods in corporate L&D, workforce development, and professional upskilling contexts. Repubit connection brings startup technology perspectives to workforce LE discussions.
role: Workforce LE practitioner; ICICLE Workforce MIG co-chair

name: DJ Jaeger
description: Co-chair of the Workforce MIG within IEEE ICICLE, alongside John Costa. Applies learning engineering principles to workforce development and corporate learning programs.
role: Workforce learning practitioner; ICICLE Workforce MIG co-chair

name: John Liu
description: Principal investigator of the MIT Learning Engineering and Practice (LEAP) group, applying data science and LE methods to improve engineering curricula at scale. Develops methods for identifying prerequisite gaps and mapping concept dependencies from student performance data in large MIT engineering courses.
affiliation: MIT, Mechanical Engineering / MIT LEAP Group
role: Engineering education researcher; MIT LEAP Group PI; data-driven curriculum researcher

name: Norman Bier
description: Director of CMU's Open Learning Initiative (OLI), which produces open online courses shown to produce learning outcomes comparable to semester-length courses in half the time. Pioneered continuous data-driven redesign of online courses. Bridges the Simon Initiative's research infrastructure with open educational access goals. Active ICICLE contributor.
affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University — Open Learning Initiative
role: OLI Director; evidence-based online course design practitioner; open educational resources leader

name: Scotty D. Craig
description: Professor and leader in ASU Learning Engineering Institute initiatives. Systematically investigates design principles for virtual humans and pedagogical agents in learning environments. Demonstrated that vocal quality, aesthetic design, and agent persona significantly affect learner cognitive load and outcomes. Co-authored the Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model. Bridges human factors engineering with instructional technology research.
role: Human factors researcher; virtual humans and pedagogical agents specialist

name: Herbert A. Simon
description: Nobel laureate in Economics (1978) who coined the term 'learning engineer' in his 1967 Educational Record essay 'The Job of a College President'. Argued that teaching is a domain of expertise separate from subject-matter knowledge, requiring its own scientific grounding. Co-developed ACT cognitive theory with John Anderson, providing the scientific basis for cognitive tutors. His broader work on bounded rationality, problem solving, and cognitive simulation laid the intellectual foundation for ITS.
role: Originator of the 'learning engineer' concept; Nobel laureate

name: John R. Anderson
description: Cognitive scientist and architect of ACT-R (Adaptive Control of Thought–Rational), the dominant cognitive architecture for modeling skill learning. Creator of the LISP Tutor (1983), the first empirically validated intelligent tutoring system. Co-developed the Cognitive Tutor for algebra, deployed in 2,600+ schools to half a million students. Demonstrated that ITS-based tutoring produces learning gains equivalent to 1-to-1 human tutoring.
role: Cognitive architect; creator of ACT-R; pioneer of Cognitive Tutors

name: Allen Newell
description: Pioneer of artificial intelligence and cognitive science. Co-developed SOAR — a unified theory of cognition — and with Herbert Simon created Logic Theorist (1956) and General Problem Solver (1957), the first AI programs. Established the 'physical symbol system hypothesis', the theoretical bridge between computation and cognition. SOAR's chunking mechanism provided an explicit computational theory of procedural skill acquisition.
role: Co-creator of SOAR cognitive architecture; pioneer of AI and cognitive science

name: John Sweller
description: Educational psychologist who formalized Cognitive Load Theory (CLT), distinguishing intrinsic, extraneous, and germane cognitive load. Established that working memory constraints must drive instructional design decisions. CLT-derived principles (worked examples effect, split-attention effect, redundancy effect) are now standard LE design rules. Provided the scientific justification for explicit, structured instruction over unguided discovery.
role: Developer of Cognitive Load Theory

name: Ann L. Brown
description: Educational psychologist and pioneer of design-based research (DBR) and collaborative learning. Her 'Design Experiments' (JLS, 1992) is the most-cited paper in the learning sciences. Co-created 'Fostering Communities of Learners', an influential model for scaffolded inquiry-based learning environments. Demonstrated how engineered classroom environments can simultaneously serve as research sites.
role: Pioneer of design-based research and collaborative learning environments

name: Marlene Scardamalia
description: Pioneer of knowledge-building communities and computer-supported collaborative learning. Co-developed Knowledge Forum (formerly CSILE), the first networked collaborative knowledge-building environment. With Bereiter, theorized the distinction between 'knowledge telling' and 'knowledge transforming' in writing and learning. Knowledge-building pedagogy influenced the design of modern collaborative learning systems and MOOCs.
role: Pioneer of knowledge-building communities and CSCL

name: Chris Dede
description: Pioneer of immersive learning environments and hybrid education. 'Immersive interfaces for engagement and learning' (Science, 2009) has thousands of citations. Co-edited Learning Engineering for Online Education (Routledge, 2019) — the field's first major book. Post-pandemic work framed hybrid learning not as emergency measure but as optimal future state. Directed multiple NSF and DoE grants applying LE principles to K-12 and higher education.
role: Pioneer of immersive learning environments; hybrid education researcher

name: Neil Heffernan
description: Director of Learning Sciences and Technologies at WPI and creator of ASSISTments, a free ITS serving 500,000+ students and 20,000+ teachers. Built E-TRIALS within ASSISTments — a platform for running randomized controlled experiments at scale in real classrooms. Pioneered 'crowdsourcing' of instructional design through teacher-authored hint sequences.
role: Creator of ASSISTments; large-scale LE experimentation pioneer

name: Karen Willcox
description: Led MIT's Online Education Policy Initiative (OEPI), producing the 2016 catalyst report that re-introduced 'learning engineer' to higher education discourse. Argued that MOOCs and digital scale require a new professional class with learning science and engineering expertise. Connected learning engineering to computational science methodologies from aerospace engineering. Now at UT Austin's Oden Institute for Computational Engineering.
affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, Oden Institute
role: MIT OEPI lead; LE in higher education advocate; computational science-to-education bridge

name: Albert T. Corbett
description: Co-developer of Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT), the dominant probabilistic student model in ITS. Co-designed the ACT Programming Tutor and Algebra Cognitive Tutor with Anderson and Koedinger. Known for systematic empirical work on learning curves, error analysis, and mastery criteria in cognitive tutors.
role: Co-developer of Bayesian Knowledge Tracing; ITS architecture pioneer

name: Robert R. Hoffman
description: Pioneer of cognitive task analysis and knowledge elicitation methodology at IHMC. Co-authored the field-defining practitioner guide Working Minds (MIT Press, 2006) with Gary Klein and Beth Crandall. Developed proficiency scale frameworks extending the Dreyfus model for training design. Active IEEE ICICLE contributor who brought human factors expertise research tradition to the LE field.
affiliation: Institute for Human and Machine Cognition (IHMC), Pensacola FL
role: Cognitive task analysis pioneer; expertise and proficiency researcher; knowledge elicitation methodologist

name: Gary Klein
description: Creator of the Recognition-Primed Decision (RPD) model and Critical Decision Method. Founder of the naturalistic decision-making (NDM) field, which informs how experts are trained in high-stakes environments. Author of Sources of Power (MIT Press, 1998), widely read by LE practitioners for insight into expertise-based training design. Connects expertise science directly to instructional design for high-stakes professional contexts.
role: Naturalistic decision-making pioneer; Recognition-Primed Decision model creator

name: Vincent Aleven
description: CMU HCII researcher who developed Example-Tracing Tutors — a new ITS paradigm requiring no cognitive model, built by demonstrating solution paths. Led research on the assistance dilemma (when to give vs. withhold help) and metacognitive scaffolding in ITS. Co-developed deployed math tutors including Lynnette.
role: ITS researcher; pioneer of example-tracing tutors and help-seeking scaffolding

name: Carolyn Rosé
description: CMU researcher who applied NLP to analyze and scaffold collaborative learning conversations in real time. Developed the BASILICA architecture for conversational agents and the Bazaar collaborative learning platform. Bridged computational linguistics and CSCL, founding a distinct NLP-for-learning subfield.
affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University, HCII / Language Technologies Institute
role: NLP researcher; pioneer of computational analysis of collaborative learning discourse

name: Jack Mostow
description: Created Project LISTEN — the first ITS that listens to children read aloud and provides real-time corrective feedback using automated speech recognition. Developed data-driven methods for evaluating ITS effectiveness using log data from deployed systems.
role: Pioneer of AI-powered reading tutors; data-driven ITS evaluation methods

name: Rod Roscoe
description: ASU Learning Engineering Institute researcher who co-led the Writing Pal ITS — a game-based writing tutor with automated essay scoring and strategy training. Investigated usability and metacognitive dimensions of writing intelligent tutoring systems and how learners interact with and trust automated feedback.
affiliation: Arizona State University, Learning Engineering Institute
role: Writing ITS researcher; human factors in automated writing feedback

name: Robert K. Atkinson
description: ASU researcher who demonstrated that optimized sequencing of worked examples using animated pedagogical agents improves learning. Applied cognitive load theory principles to the design of example-based instruction and investigated the guidance fading effect across different instructional formats.
role: Instructional design researcher; worked examples and pedagogical agents

name: Mina C. Johnson-Glenberg
description: Directs the Embodied Games Lab at ASU. Demonstrated that embodied interaction in mixed reality environments improves STEM concept learning. Developed design guidelines for immersive learning environments based on embodied cognition theory. Positioned XR as a rigorous LE domain with measurable outcome metrics.
affiliation: Arizona State University, Embodied Games Lab
role: XR and embodied learning researcher; mixed reality STEM education

name: Arthur C. Graesser
description: Created AutoTutor — an ITS that conducts Socratic dialogue in natural language, covering physics, computer literacy, and other domains. Showed AutoTutor produces deep learning gains comparable to expert human tutors. Co-developed Coh-Metrix for analyzing textual cohesion and readability. Co-founded the discourse-based approach to ITS.
affiliation: University of Memphis, Institute for Intelligent Systems
role: AutoTutor developer; discourse analysis and comprehension in ITS

name: Philip I. Pavlik Jr.
description: Developed Performance Factors Analysis (PFA) — a student model accounting for both practice and forgetting. Derived an activation-based model of the spacing effect and created algorithms for optimal practice scheduling. Contributed to Learning Factors Analysis (LFA) for data-driven discovery of knowledge components.
affiliation: University of Memphis, Institute for Intelligent Systems
role: Cognitive modeling researcher; spacing and retrieval practice optimization in ITS

name: Ashok K. Goel
description: Created Jill Watson — one of the first AI teaching assistants deployed at scale in a university course, handling 40,000+ student questions with near-human accuracy. Applied case-based reasoning to educational question-answering. Led the AI4K12 initiative developing national guidelines for K-12 AI literacy education.
affiliation: Georgia Institute of Technology, School of Interactive Computing
role: AI educator; creator of Jill Watson AI teaching assistant

name: Sidney K. D'Mello
description: Demonstrated that confusion, when productively resolved, benefits deep learning in ITS contexts. Developed multimodal sensors (eye tracking, posture, facial expression) for real-time affect detection during learning. Mapped the dynamics of affective states (boredom, confusion, frustration, flow) during complex learning tasks.
affiliation: University of Colorado Boulder, Institute of Cognitive Science
role: Affective computing researcher; emotion detection in intelligent tutoring

name: Justin Reich
description: Authored Failure to Disrupt (MIT Press, 2020) — the most rigorous critique of MOOC platforms' failure to improve educational equity despite scale. Demonstrated empirically that MOOCs disproportionately benefit already-advantaged learners. Leads MIT Teaching Systems Lab, studying how teacher learning and practice change with educational technology.
role: EdTech researcher; critic of MOOC hype; evidence-based online learning advocate

name: Bruce M. McLaren
description: CMU HCII research scientist who designed Decimal Point — a math game for decimal numbers shown to outperform conventional tutoring software in controlled trials. Investigated optimal sequencing of worked examples and tutored problem solving, and developed adaptive worked-example and ITS hybrid approaches for fraction and decimal learning.
role: ITS researcher; learning games and worked-example sequencing in mathematics

name: Ken Holstein
description: CMU HCII researcher who developed real-time multimodal classroom systems giving teachers live student-struggle signals during ITS sessions. Demonstrated that teacher-AI complementarity — not replacement — yields better outcomes. Published the most-cited work on equity and fairness in AI-driven educational systems.
role: Human-AI complementarity researcher; equity in adaptive learning; real-time teacher orchestration

name: Erin Walker
description: Developed ITS scaffolding systems that support peer tutoring in computer science, bridging collaborative and adaptive learning. Showed intelligent support for peer tutoring outperforms unscaffolded peer tutoring and approaches one-on-one ITS. Bridges CMU HCII cognitive tutor lineage with ASU's collaborative learning traditions.
affiliation: University of Pittsburgh (formerly Arizona State University; Carnegie Mellon University, HCII)
role: ITS and peer-tutoring researcher; collaborative learning scaffolding

name: Danielle S. McNamara
